title: Lancashire Hotpot with Kidneys
subtitle: Braised lamb shoulder and kidneys under a potato crust, the classic Lancashire baked casserole
excerpt: Lancashire hotpot is a baked casserole of lamb and root vegetables topped with overlapping potato slices that brown to a crust. The kidneys are traditional and give the gravy a depth that lamb alone does not achieve. It requires 2 hours in the oven and almost no active work.
sourceNotes: Traditional Lancashire. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: hotpot from mutton and kidneys. Traditional Lancashire mill workers' dish. Elizabeth David, English Bread and Yeast Cookery (1977) and Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): regional British dishes.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before the final uncovered browning stage. Defrost overnight and reheat at 180°C for 30-35 minutes, uncovering for the final 15 minutes to re-crisp the potato top.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble completely a day ahead and refrigerate. Bake from cold, adding 20 minutes to the covered cooking time.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven for covered stage, then 190°C to brown the potato top

glossaryTerms.definition: A baked casserole of lamb or mutton with root vegetables, topped with overlapping sliced potato and cooked in a deep earthenware pot or casserole. The dish originates in Lancashire, where it was a staple of textile mill workers' households: cheap to assemble, requiring no attention during its long bake in the bread oven after the day's bread was done.

Each kidney must be halved and the white fatty core removed before cooking. Split the kidney lengthways, then cut a V-shape into the flat cut side to remove the pale, rubbery suet core entirely. The core has an unpleasant chewy texture; removing it takes about 30 seconds per kidney and transforms the result.

content.text: is assembled raw and cooked in one pot. There is no pre-browning of the meat: the lamb and vegetables are layered directly in the casserole with flour, stock, and aromatics, and the long bake in the oven does all the work.
carefully: the white suet core must be removed from each half before they go in. Left in, it gives a harsh bitterness to the gravy; removed, the kidneys add only depth and a faint irony richness.

items.prepNote: medium, peeled and sliced 3 mm thick
melted, for brushing the potato top

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C (150°C fan). Dust the
. In a large deep casserole, layer the meat with the onion, carrot,
and the Worcestershire sauce if using. Arrange the sliced potato in overlapping layers over the top, seasoning as you go. Brush with

Cover the casserole tightly and bake for 1.5 hours. Uncover, increase the oven to 190°C (170°C fan), and bake for a further 30 minutes until the potato top is golden and the lamb is completely tender. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

rows.symptom: Potato top is pale and soft, not golden
rows.cause: Lid was not removed for the final stage, or oven not hot enough
rows.fix: Remove the lid and turn the oven up to 200°C for the last 20-25 minutes. Brush the potato with a little extra butter if it looks dry.

rows.cause: Too much stock, or the flour dusting was skipped
rows.fix: The flour on the meat thickens the gravy during the long bake. Ensure it is well dusted. If still thin, pour the gravy into a small saucepan and reduce on the hob before serving.

rows.symptom: Lamb is still tough after 2 hours
rows.cause: Lamb shoulder varies; some pieces need longer
rows.fix: Replace the lid and return to a 160°C oven for a further 30-40 minutes. The potato top can be browned in a final blast at the end.

rows.cause: The suet core was not fully removed
rows.fix: Cut a generous V into each kidney half to ensure the pale core is completely removed.

content.text: Lancashire hotpot is one of the most enduring dishes in British regional cooking. It was made by mill workers' wives who assembled the pot in the morning and left it in the bread oven to cook slowly through the day. The kidney is the traditional addition: a practical way of making the meat go further while adding flavour. Serve with pickled red cabbage, which cuts through the richness of the gravy.
